metadata.summary: Poetry is the standard for Python dependency management: deterministic builds via poetry.lock, isolated virtual environments, and streamlined PyPI publishing.

content.title: Poetry Project Setup
content.summary: Poetry is the standard for Python dependency management: deterministic builds via poetry.lock, isolated virtual environments, and streamlined PyPI publishing. Always commit poetry.lock; always use --sync in CI; never mix pip and poetry in the same project.

why.p: Without a lockfile, two agents on different machines resolve different dependency trees and produce non-reproducible builds. Poetry's solver generates a lockfile that pins every transitive dependency. The lock must be committed and treated as a reviewable artifact — the lockfile diff is the visible record of what changed.

list.item: Starting any new Python project (service, library, CLI).
Managing complex dependency trees requiring reproducible builds.
Publishing packages to PyPI or private registries.
Agent-managed repos where dependency changes must go through --dry-run + human approval.
Bootstrapping a new Python service or library that will be agent-managed (Claude Code as primary contributor).
Locking dependency closures so a CI agent and a local agent produce byte-identical environments.
Wrapping a Django/FastAPI repo where you want poetry add / poetry remove to be the only mutation surface for pyproject.toml.
Publishing a wheel to PyPI/private index with token-based auth (Poetry handles upload state without manual twine).
Throwaway scripts or notebooks — uv pip install or pipx run is faster.
Projects already standardized on pip-tools, uv, or pdm — don't migrate for novelty.
Docker images built from requirements.txt with no solver runtime needed — export from Poetry at build time and use pip in the final stage.
Monorepos with Pants/Bazel workspace tooling — Poetry has no first-class workspace concept.
Containers built from requirements.txt with no need for solver runtime — keep pip; export from Poetry only at build time.
Monorepos with native workspace tooling (Pants, Bazel) — Poetry has no first-class workspace concept (until 2.0 plugins).

rule.statement: Always commit poetry.lock. There is no situation where a service or library should omit it from version control.
rule.rationale: Without the lock, two agents on different machines resolve different transitive dependency trees, producing non-reproducible builds and subtle environment bugs.

rule.statement: Run poetry install --sync --no-interaction in CI. The --sync flag removes packages that left the lockfile, preventing zombie dependencies.
rule.rationale: Without --sync, deleted dependencies accumulate in CI environments across runs and cause false-positive test passes.

rule.statement: Never run poetry update without arguments in a shared repo. It rewrites the entire lockfile, overwriting another developer's or agent's staged resolver result. Use poetry update <package> to bump a single package.
rule.rationale: Full lockfile rewrites are destructive to concurrent work and produce unreviewed dependency changes.

section.name: dependency-groups

rule.statement: Split dependencies into groups: main, dev, test, docs. Mark docs as optional = true so production installs don't pull mkdocs. Keep production deps minimal.
rule.rationale: Smaller production dependency trees mean smaller Docker images, fewer CVE surface area, and faster pip install in the final stage.

example.title: Dependency Group Organization
example.code:
# Production poetry add fastapi uvicorn sqlalchemy
# Dev tools poetry add --group dev ruff mypy black pytest
# Optional docs poetry add --group docs --optional mkdocs mkdocs-material
# Install specific groups in CI poetry install --only main poetry install --with dev,test

section.reference: Full pyproject.toml with groups and tool configs

rule.statement: Use tight Python version constraints (python = "^3.11", not ">=3.8"). Broad ranges slow the solver and produce hostile error messages for conflicting extras.
rule.rationale: The Poetry solver has quadratic complexity in constraint width. Tight Python pins halve the search space.

rule.statement: Before any poetry add or poetry remove, run poetry check --lock. If the lockfile is dirty, resolve that first before adding new dependencies.
rule.rationale: Adding a package when the lockfile is dirty silently merges the dirty state into the new lock, creating an unreviewed dependency change.

section.name: docker-multi-stage-pattern

rule.statement: In Docker builds, use a builder stage to run poetry export, then a final stage with pip install -r requirements.txt. Never run poetry install in the final production stage — it brings the Poetry runtime into the image.
rule.rationale: Poetry adds ~100MB to the image and is not needed at runtime. The export + pip pattern produces minimal images.

example.title: Multi-Stage Docker Build
example.code:
FROM python:3.11-slim AS builder WORKDIR /app RUN pip install poetry==1.8.5 COPY pyproject.toml poetry.lock ./ RUN poetry export -f requirements.txt --output requirements.txt --without-hashes
FROM python:3.11-slim WORKDIR /app COPY --from=builder /app/requirements.txt . RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt COPY src/ ./src/ CMD ["python", "-m", "my_project.main"]
example.note: Pin the poetry version in the builder stage. poetry shell is removed in Poetry 2.0 — never call it in Dockerfiles.

section.reference: Full multi-stage Dockerfile for Poetry projects

rule.statement: Use snok/install-poetry@v1 in CI and cache the .venv directory keyed by OS + Python version + poetry.lock hash. This reduces install time from minutes to seconds on cache hit.
rule.rationale: Poetry's dependency resolution is the slowest CI step. Caching the resolved environment avoids re-running the solver on every push.

example.title: GitHub Actions Poetry Setup with Cache

rule.statement: Use the POETRY_PYPI_TOKEN_PYPI environment variable in CI, not poetry config pypi-token.pypi $TOKEN. The env var is process-scoped; the config command writes to ~/.config/pypoetry/auth.toml which persists across runs in shared sandboxes.
rule.rationale: Token written to auth.toml in a shared CI environment is accessible to other jobs. Process-scoped env var leaks nothing beyond the current step.

list.item: poetry new my-project — create new project with standard layout
poetry init — initialize in existing directory
poetry install --sync --no-interaction — install from lock, prune zombies
poetry add --group dev pytest ruff — add dev dependency
poetry update <package> — bump single package only
poetry export -f requirements.txt --output requirements.txt — export for Docker
poetry check --lock — verify lockfile is current
poetry env info --path — show virtual environment path

antipattern.description: Agent calls poetry shell in headless CI or Docker.
antipattern.reason: poetry shell hangs forever waiting for a TTY. Poetry 2.0 removed it entirely. Always use poetry run <cmd> from agents, never poetry shell.

antipattern.description: Agent uses pip install to add a package to a Poetry-managed project.
antipattern.reason: pip install bypasses Poetry's solver and does not update pyproject.toml or poetry.lock. The next poetry install will remove the package.

antipattern.description: Agent writes "^0.1" expecting it to mean <1.0.0.
antipattern.reason: In Poetry, ^0.1 means >=0.1.0,<0.2.0 (not <1.0.0). The caret rule applies to the leftmost non-zero digit. Always run poetry add --dry-run and read back the resolved range before committing.

antipattern.description: Agent calls poetry add X when the lockfile is dirty (uncommitted local changes).
antipattern.reason: Poetry silently merges the dirty state into the new lock. Run poetry check --lock before any add/remove.

antipattern.description: Agent uses poetry self add instead of pip install to install a Poetry plugin.
antipattern.reason: Actually reversed: pip install into the Poetry interpreter corrupts Poetry's own deps. Use poetry self add for plugins. The antipattern is using pip for Poetry's own environment.

section.name: solver-limitations
section.note: Solver can hang on large constraint sets (e.g., many numpy/scipy/torch extras); poetry lock --no-update is mandatory for partial bumps.
poetry install in Docker is ~2-3x slower than pip install -r requirements.txt; production images should poetry export at build time and use plain pip.
poetry shell was removed in Poetry 2.0 (replaced by poetry env activate echo + manual source); older docs and many agent scripts still call poetry shell and break.
poetry update without arguments rewrites the entire lockfile — destructive when a teammate has staged a different solver result.
Hash mismatch errors from a partially populated cache (~/.cache/pypoetry) require poetry cache clear --all pypi to recover.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A code-writing subagent proposes the dependency change, then a separate command executor runs poetry add ... --dry-run, parses the diff, and only after human/coordinator approval runs the real command. The lockfile diff is treated as a reviewable artifact (committed alongside pyproject.toml). For CI, agents should call poetry install --sync --no-interaction --no-root so removed deps get pruned and the run is reproducible.

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — drives task-level changes; can own a TASK that says "add httpx and pin to ^0.27".
password-scrubber-agent — sanity-check before publishing; ensures no pypi-token, repo URLs with creds, or private index credentials slipped into pyproject.toml.
A purpose-built dependency-bumper subagent (not yet in repo) for routine poetry update --dry-run → PR loop.
Pin Python with a tight constraint (python = "^3.11", not ">=3.8"); broader ranges slow the solver and produce hostile error messages.
Always use --sync in CI install — it removes packages that left the lockfile, preventing zombie deps.
Split dev, test, docs groups; mark docs as optional = true so production installs don't pull mkdocs.
Commit poetry.lock. Period. There is no situation where a library should omit it.
When publishing a library, set [tool.poetry.dependencies] ranges loosely (^x.y) but keep poetry.lock for the dev workflow. Don't ship the lock to PyPI.
Use poetry self add for plugins, never pip install into the Poetry interpreter — pip will silently corrupt Poetry's own deps.
Combine with pre-commit running ruff check --fix, ruff format, and a poetry check --lock hook that fails on dirty lockfile.
For Docker: multi-stage build, run poetry export --without-hashes -o requirements.txt --with main, install via pip in final stage, keep image small and free of Poetry runtime.
An agent calling poetry add X when the lockfile is dirty (someone added Y locally and didn't commit) will silently lock Y too. Always run poetry check --lock before any add/remove.
poetry shell in headless CI hangs forever waiting for a tty. Use poetry run <cmd> from agents — never shell.
Network blips during poetry lock corrupt the cache; agent retries should poetry cache clear --all pypi --no-interaction between attempts.

Some plugins write absolute paths into poetry.lock (develop = true editable installs). Agents that copy lockfiles between machines will produce non-reproducible installs — use --no-editable when it matters.
Version-string mistakes are the #1 agent error: ^0.1 means >=0.1.0,<0.2.0 (NOT <1.0.0). Have the agent always run poetry add --dry-run and read back the resolved range before commit.
Token leak risk: poetry config pypi-token.pypi $TOKEN writes to ~/.config/pypoetry/auth.toml. Agents in shared sandboxes must use POETRY_PYPI_TOKEN_PYPI env var instead, which is process-scoped.
poetry install without --no-root will try to install the current project as a package; for service repos (no setup.py install needed) always pass --no-root to avoid spurious failures.
Poetry 2.0 changed plugin and command behavior (e.g., removed shell, changed lock --no-update to default). Pin Poetry version (pipx install 'poetry==1.8.5' or 2.x explicitly) in CI to avoid drift.
poetry — Solver, env mgmt, build, publish. Install: curl -sSL https://install.python-poetry.org | python3 -
poetry-plugin-export — Emit requirements.txt for Docker. Install: poetry self add poetry-plugin-export
poetry-plugin-bundle — Bundle env into a venv directory. Install: poetry self add poetry-plugin-bundle
pipx — Isolate Poetry itself from project venvs. Install: apt install pipx
tomlq / dasel — Read/edit pyproject.toml from scripts. https://github.com/TomWright/dasel
pip-audit — CVE scan against the lockfile. Install: pipx install pip-audit then poetry export | pip-audit -r /dev/stdin
deptry — Detect unused/missing imports vs declared deps. Install: pipx install deptry
uv — Drop-in faster resolver/installer; can read Poetry projects. https://github.com/astral-sh/uv

template.purpose: Full pyproject.toml with dependency groups, tool configs (ruff, mypy, pytest)
Multi-stage Docker build: poetry export in builder, pip install in final stage
